title: Forge the Polis: Rival of Athens and Sparta
description: Build and govern a Greek polis while balancing democracy, oligarchy, military strength, trade, philosophy, citizenship, and law.

coverImage.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

termDefinitions.polis: A Greek city-state made up of a city and its surrounding countryside.
termDefinitions.city-state: An independent city and the surrounding land it controls.
termDefinitions.Athens: A Greek city-state known for democracy, naval power, philosophy, and its Golden Age.
termDefinitions.Sparta: A Greek city-state known for military discipline, strong land forces, and an oligarchic government.
termDefinitions.democracy: A form of government in which citizens participate in decision-making.
termDefinitions.direct democracy: A system where citizens vote directly on laws and policies instead of choosing representatives.
termDefinitions.oligarchy: A government ruled by a small group of powerful people.
termDefinitions.tyrant: A ruler who takes power outside the normal laws or traditions, often ruling alone.
termDefinitions.citizen: A legally recognized member of a polis with rights and responsibilities.
termDefinitions.assembly: A group of citizens who meet to debate and vote on laws or policies.
termDefinitions.agora: The public marketplace and gathering place in a Greek polis.
termDefinitions.acropolis: A high fortified area of a Greek city-state, often used for temples and defense.
termDefinitions.hoplite: A heavily armed Greek foot soldier.
termDefinitions.phalanx: A tight military formation used by Greek hoplites standing shield to shield.
termDefinitions.trireme: A fast Greek warship powered by rows of oarsmen.
termDefinitions.navy: A military force made up of ships used to fight at sea.
termDefinitions.Peloponnesus: The southern peninsula of Greece where Sparta was located.
termDefinitions.Aegean Sea: The sea east of mainland Greece that connected Greek city-states through trade and travel.
termDefinitions.colonization: The process of settling new lands while maintaining cultural or economic ties to the original city-state.
termDefinitions.trade: The exchange of goods, ideas, and resources.
termDefinitions.Delian League: An alliance of Greek city-states led by Athens after the Persian Wars.
termDefinitions.Persian Wars: A series of wars between Greek city-states and the Persian Empire.
termDefinitions.philosophy: The study of wisdom, truth, ethics, government, and the nature of life.
termDefinitions.Golden Age: A period of great cultural, artistic, political, or economic achievement.
termDefinitions.rule of law: The idea that laws apply to people and leaders, rather than power being based only on personal authority.
termDefinitions.social hierarchy: A ranking system in society where some groups have more status, power, or rights than others.

teacherNotes: This vocabulary-rich deep-tree adventure has students build a Greek polis that rivals Athens and Sparta. Academic vocabulary is intentionally embedded into the body text so students encounter terms in context, not only in a glossary. This file contains no hint fields and no time estimate.
reviewFocus: how geography, government, citizenship, military systems, trade, philosophy, and social hierarchy shaped Ancient Greek city-states
topicQuestion: what made a Greek polis successful?

start.title: The Valley That Could Become a Polis

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

start.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: You are Alexios, a young citizen chosen to help design a new Greek polis, or city-state.
The valley before you has three gifts: fertile farmland, a sheltered harbor on the Aegean Sea, and a rocky hill that could become an acropolis.

Settlers arrive with different dreams. Some admire Athens, where citizens gather in an assembly and practice direct democracy. Others admire Sparta, where discipline, hoplites, and the phalanx create military strength.
Your challenge is not to copy Athens or Sparta. Your challenge is to build a polis strong enough to rival both.

choices.text: Build the polis around Athenian-style democracy and debate.
Build the polis around Spartan-style discipline and military training.
Combine democratic citizenship with military readiness.
Invite philosophers, generals, farmers, and merchants to debate first.

athenian-opening.title: The Agora Fills With Voices

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

athenian-opening.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: You begin in the agora, the public marketplace where citizens trade goods, share news, and debate laws.
A sailor argues that a strong navy of triremes will protect the harbor. A farmer argues that laws must protect landowners. A potter argues that artisans deserve a voice too.
Democracy gives citizens power, but it also fills the polis with argument.

choices.text: Expand the assembly so more male citizens can vote directly.
Create a council of wealthy and experienced leaders.
Build the harbor economy and a navy of triremes.
Write a mixed constitution with assembly, council, and elected generals.

body: You place the training ground below the acropolis so every child can see the soldiers drill.
Veterans teach young men to stand shoulder to shoulder as hoplites in a phalanx, the tight battle formation that gives Greek armies strength.
The polis becomes disciplined quickly, but merchants and poets worry that military order may leave little room for freedom.

choices.text: Expand the assembly so more male citizens can vote directly.

body: You design the polis around two ideals: the voice of Athens and the discipline of Sparta.
Citizens will debate in the assembly, but they will also train as hoplites. Merchants may trade through the harbor, but soldiers will guard the roads.
Balance sounds wise, but it is difficult. Each faction thinks its own value should come first.

choices.text: Expand the assembly so more male citizens can vote directly.
Create a council of wealthy and experienced leaders.
Build the harbor economy and a navy of triremes.
Write a mixed constitution with assembly, council, and elected generals.

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

body: In the agora, philosophers, generals, farmers, merchants, and artisans gather beneath painted columns.

One speaker praises direct democracy because citizens should govern themselves. Another praises oligarchy because experienced elites may make faster decisions. A third warns that a desperate polis may turn to a tyrant.
The debate teaches the first lesson of politics: every government solves one problem while creating another.

choices.text: Expand the assembly so more male citizens can vote directly.
Create a council of wealthy and experienced leaders.
Build the harbor economy and a navy of triremes.
Write a mixed constitution with assembly, council, and elected generals.

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

expanded-assembly.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: The assembly expands. More citizens gather on the hillside to vote on laws, taxes, war, and public projects.
Citizens feel pride because the polis belongs to them. Yet speakers with loud voices begin persuading crowds with emotion rather than evidence.
Direct democracy requires more than voting. It requires judgment.

choices.text: Teach rhetoric, history, and philosophy to prepare citizens.
Create written rules for debate and voting.
Pay citizens for public service so poorer citizens can attend.
Let popular speakers control the assembly.

oligarchic-council.title: Rule of the Few

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

oligarchic-council.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: A council of wealthy landowners and experienced soldiers takes control of major decisions.
They call the system an oligarchy, rule by a few, and claim it protects the polis from reckless crowds.
Farmers, sailors, and artisans begin asking why they must fight for a city-state they cannot govern.

choices.text: Allow the council but create public courts for citizens.
Give some assembly power back to ordinary citizens.
Use soldiers to silence complaints.
Offer economic opportunity to calm resentment.

mixed-constitution.title: The Balanced Constitution

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

mixed-constitution.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: You create a mixed constitution. The assembly votes on major questions, a council prepares laws, and elected generals lead in wartime.
Written laws are displayed near the agora so citizens can see the rule of law with their own eyes.
The system is slower than tyranny, freer than oligarchy, and more organized than a shouting crowd.

choices.text: Strengthen public courts and written law.
Train citizens in both debate and military service.
Use the constitution to build alliances with other poleis.
Let factions rewrite rules for their own advantage.

body: The harbor becomes the beating heart of the polis.
Triremes protect merchant ships carrying pottery, olive oil, wine, grain, and timber across the Aegean Sea.
Trade brings wealth and new ideas, but it also makes the city-state dependent on sea routes.

choices.text: Build diplomatic trade agreements with other poleis.
Force weaker cities to pay tribute.
Use harbor wealth for public works.
Neglect farmland because trade seems richer.

body: Teachers instruct citizens in rhetoric, philosophy, history, and civic duty.
Young men learn to speak in the assembly, question evidence, and understand the laws of the polis.
A democracy becomes stronger when citizens can think beyond their own anger.

choices.text: Open civic education to poorer citizens as well.
Invite philosophers from Athens to teach ethics.
Focus education only on wealthy families.
Use education to prepare citizens for war debates.

body: The laws of the polis are carved into stone and displayed near the agora.
Citizens can read the rules for taxes, military service, trials, property, and appeals.
The rule of law does not make everyone equal, but it makes power less secret.

choices.text: Create fair courts for citizens accused of crimes.
Apply laws differently based on wealth and status.
Use written law to limit generals and councils.
Ignore the laws during crisis.

paid-citizenship.title: The Poor Citizen's Seat

image.alt: An Ancient Greek polis with an agora, acropolis, harbor, hoplites, and citizens debating government

paid-citizenship.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: The polis offers small payments so poorer citizens can leave work and attend the assembly or serve on juries.
Supporters say this strengthens democracy. Critics say it will make citizens dependent on public money.
The question becomes who can truly be a citizen if only the wealthy have time to govern.

choices.text: Fund participation with harbor taxes.
Limit payments to jury service only.
End payments after wealthy citizens complain.
Use participation to build unity before war.

body: A popular speaker named Thales fills the assembly with promises.
He says the polis can have low taxes, high pay, stronger walls, and victory over enemies all at once.
The crowd loves him. Philosophers warn that democracy can be weakened when citizens stop asking hard questions.

choices.text: Require public speakers to defend claims with evidence.
Let Thales lead policy because he is popular.
Create procedures to slow dangerous votes.
Turn to a strong leader to silence him.

body: The polis gives temporary power to Damon, a popular general who promises quick decisions.
At first, he builds walls, organizes taxes, and settles disputes. Many citizens cheer the efficiency.
Then critics disappear from the agora, and people begin whispering the word tyrant.

choices.text: Force Damon to step down after the crisis.
Let Damon keep power because the city feels orderly.
Use Damon's authority to expand the military.
Rewrite the constitution to limit future emergency powers.

body: Citizens debate in the assembly and drill in the phalanx.
They learn that freedom requires responsibility, and military discipline requires trust.
The polis begins developing a shared identity stronger than family, class, or neighborhood.

choices.text: Use shared service to unite rich and poor citizens.
Exclude poorer citizens from full honor.
Prepare citizen-soldiers for a Persian threat.

body: Philosophers gather beneath olive trees to debate justice, virtue, citizenship, and the best form of government.
One asks whether democracy is wise if citizens are uneducated. Another asks whether Sparta's order is admirable if freedom is limited.
The polis begins to see philosophy not as idle talk, but as a tool for shaping public life.

choices.text: Use philosophy to strengthen civic education.
Create an academy funded by the polis.
Let philosophers criticize leaders openly.
Silence philosophers who question military policy.

Citizens see their money return as public works. Foreign visitors begin comparing your polis to Athens during its Golden Age.

elite-education-power.pathFocus: Key terms for this route: polis, city-state, Athens, Sparta, democracy, direct democracy, oligarchy, tyrant, citizen, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, navy, Peloponnesus, Aegean Sea, colonization, trade, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy.
body: Your polis becomes powerful, wealthy, and feared.
Tribute, alliances, conquest, or naval pressure bring influence, but neighboring poleis resent your success.
You rival Athens and Sparta, but you also inherit their problem: power creates enemies.
elite-education-power.teachingSummary: This ending shows that Greek city-states could gain influence through military and naval power, but expansion often created resentment and instability.
elite-education-power.keyTermReview: Vocabulary review: This adventure connects the Greek polis to geography, government, military systems, trade, and culture. Students compare Athens and Sparta while building their own city-state. The story intentionally uses terms such as democracy, oligarchy, assembly, agora, acropolis, hoplite, phalanx, trireme, colonization, Delian League, Persian Wars, philosophy, Golden Age, rule of law, and social hierarchy in context.
reflectionQuestions: Which choices most shaped your polis, and why?
How did your government compare to Athens and Sparta?
Which key term best explains your ending? Use evidence from the adventure.
How did geography influence your economy, military, and political choices?
Was your polis stronger because of democracy, military discipline, trade, philosophy, or balance?

body: Your polis becomes known for public works, education, trade, philosophy, and civic pride.
The agora is busy, the harbor is safe, and citizens feel connected to the laws they helped shape.
Your city-state enters a Golden Age remembered for both practical strength and human achievement.
elite-education-prosperity.teachingSummary: This ending shows how prosperity, trade, education, public works, and philosophy could help a Greek polis develop a Golden Age.

body: The warning signs were ignored.
Factional politics, class tension, military ambition, and rivalry weaken the polis from within.
Like many Greek city-states, your polis learns that division can destroy even a brilliant society.
